title: Retrieval as a Tool, Not a Fixed Step
concept: Letting the model decide when and what to retrieve, rather than always retrieving once before every answer.

exercises.prompt: Why do traditional, single-step RAG systems struggle with complex questions that require connecting multiple pieces of evidence?

options.text: They fetch all information upfront, making it hard to follow up on new clues discovered during reasoning.
They do not use vector embeddings at all.
They are too slow because they query the database continuously in a loop.

exercises.explain: Since this is the first lesson of the unit, we look at the core issue: traditional systems struggle with complex, multi-step queries because they fetch data once at the start without knowing what they will need next.

cards.title: Is Searching Always Necessary?
cards.body: Traditional RAG is like a researcher who must read three pre-selected articles before answering any question, even if asked 'What is 2 + 2?'. What if the AI could decide when it actually needs to look things up?

cards.title: What is Agentic Retrieval?
cards.body: Instead of forcing a search query before every single prompt, we give the LLM access to a search tool. The model evaluates the user's query and decides for itself whether it needs to call the search tool, what keywords to search for, or if it can answer directly using its own knowledge.

cards.title: The Dynamic Kitchen Helper
cards.body: Think of a standard pipeline like a rigid restaurant menu that forces you to drink soup before every entree. Letting the model decide is like giving it a personal helper: the model only asks the helper to run to the kitchen when it realizes a specific ingredient is missing.

cards.title: Smart Retrieval in Action
cards.body: When asked 'Who won the soccer match yesterday?', the LLM realizes it lacks real-time data, calls the 'search_database' tool, reads the result, and answers. If asked 'Write a poem about blue sky', it skips the tool entirely and drafts the poem instantly.

cards.title: The 'More is Better' Myth
cards.body: People assume that more context always yields better answers. In reality, retrieving irrelevant documents for simple questions wastes money, slows down the response time, and can distract or confuse the LLM.

cards.title: Fixed Pipeline vs. Agentic Choice
cards.body: Compare how information flows in a rigid pipeline versus a flexible agentic tool setup.

left.text: Always retrieves first -> Sends results to LLM -> Outputs answer.

right.text: LLM reads prompt -> Decides whether to call search tool -> Outputs answer.

exercises.prompt: How does traditional RAG differ from treating retrieval as a tool?

options.text: Traditional RAG allows the model to search multiple times recursively.
Traditional RAG always runs retrieval as a fixed step before the model generates a response.
Traditional RAG does not use a vector database.
Traditional RAG runs only when the model requests it.

exercises.explain: In traditional RAG, retrieval is a hardcoded step that runs before the model ever sees the prompt. In agentic RAG, the model decides if and when to trigger the search.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Treating retrieval as a tool allows the model to perform multiple search queries in a row if the first search didn't provide enough information.
exercises.explain: By framing retrieval as a tool, the model can choose to query the database multiple times in a loop to answer complex, multi-part questions.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to complete the description of agentic retrieval:
exercises.sentence: Instead of running a database query first, we expose search as a tool the model can choose to call.
exercises.explain: When retrieval is defined as a tool, the agent has the power to call it dynamically only when it needs extra information.

exercises.prompt: Which of these scenarios demonstrates an AI agent deciding NOT to retrieve information?

options.text: An application that automatically searches the web for the phrase 'hello' when the user types 'hello'.
A script that always retrieves the top 3 documents from a database for every user message.
An LLM agent that decides to skip calling the 'database_search' tool when asked to 'Write a creative story about a dragon'.

exercises.explain: Option C represents an agentic system deciding it does not need to search because it already knows the answer to a general knowledge question. The other options show hardcoded, rigid behaviors.

exercises.prompt: Why is treating retrieval as an agentic tool superior to a fixed pre-retrieval pipeline for complex, multi-hop questions?

options.text: It reduces the model's output token usage by skipping the reasoning steps.
It allows the agent to perform multi-step research, choosing what to search next based on previous search results.
It guarantees that the agent will never query the vector database unnecessarily.
It runs much faster because the model does not have to decide when to search.

exercises.explain: By making retrieval an optional tool that the agent can call multiple times (or not at all), the agent gains the flexibility to gather information incrementally, correcting course based on what it reads, rather than being stuck with a single, static snapshot of data collected at the very beginning.
